af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Workflow automation tool with SSO/JWT auth. Documentation is limited and enterprise-focused. SSO dependency makes agent authentication complex — requires pre-negotiated service account or machine-to-machine JWT flow. Rate limits undocumented. Limited public information available for full evaluation — enterprise access required for complete docs. Low scores reflect documentation gaps and auth complexity for agent integration.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS depends on deployment configuration. SSO/JWT auth is strong for human users but complex for service-to-service agent flows. Limited scope granularity documented. Unknown dependency hygiene. JWT tokens via secure flow but service account setup requires manual configuration. Limited public security documentation.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted or private SaaS with no public SLA information. Version stability unknown. No documented breaking change policy. Error recovery behavior undocumented. SSO session management adds reliability dependency on identity provider. Limited information available for comprehensive reliability assessment.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with decent quality (68). Flux CD is a GitOps operator for Kubernetes — manages continuous delivery from Git to cluster. Kubernetes RBAC is complex for agents — must configure service account with appropriate ClusterRole bindings. MCP server wraps kubectl/Flux CLI operations. Rate limits via Kubernetes API server (not Flux-specific). Error messages in Kubernetes event format require parsing. GitOps reconciliation is event-driven — agents trigger syncs, Flux handles the rest.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS via Kubernetes cluster (operator's responsibility). Fine-grained RBAC with least-privilege service accounts. Flux recommends read-only RBAC for monitoring agents. Flux-maintained (CNCF project). Kubernetes Secrets for credential storage — use sealed secrets or external secrets operator. GitOps model means Git repo is the control plane — secure Git access is critical. CNCF project governance adds trust.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted Kubernetes operator — uptime tied to cluster health. Flux stable versioning with CNCF backing. Good migration guides across versions. GitOps reconciliation is inherently retry-safe. Sync failures captured in Flux status conditions — agents can poll kustomization/helmrelease status. Kubernetes API server rate limiting affects agent-triggered operations. MCP server versioning separate from Flux operator versioning.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Fly.io deployment and infrastructure API. Simple auth token (from fly auth token command). App deployment is async — agents must poll machine status. Machines API preferred over Apps API for programmatic control. Volume management for persistent storage. Rate limits not well documented — underpublished. Multi-region app management. REST API primarily used via flyctl or direct calls. Webhook support limited. Good developer experience but not enterprise-hardened documentation.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Single auth token — no scope granularity or read-only mode. Fly.io maintained. No formal compliance certifications publicly listed. Auth token via environment variable. Infrastructure management has high blast radius — agents can destroy apps and volumes. Fly secrets managed separately via flyctl secrets — not exposed via API directly. Token rotation requires re-authentication.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. API is relatively stable. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Async machine operations require polling with timeouts. Rate limits underpublished — may encounter 429s unexpectedly. Machine creation not idempotent — check existence before creating. Multi-region deployment adds complexity for agent state management.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Places/location data API — venue search, details, tips, photos. Simple API key. New Places API (v3) is the current version — old v2 (legacy) deprecated. Category IDs required for filtered searches — must use Foursquare's category taxonomy. Radius-based and geocode searches. Rate limits documented by plan. Good for location-aware agent workflows. No write operations available without user context.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Foursquare maintained. No formal compliance certifications publicly listed. API key via environment variable. Location query data may carry user location privacy implications — review data retention policies. Read-only places data minimizes risk. API key in header (preferred) or query parameter.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. v3 API stable. Good backward compatibility within version. Retry guidance documented. Full GET idempotency on place queries. Rate limits by plan — monitor for free tier exhaustion. Place IDs are stable (FSQ_ID format). Geocode-based searches may return slightly different results as dataset updates. API deprecation timeline for v2 well-communicated.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Accounting and invoicing SaaS. OAuth2 only — no API key option makes agent auth complex (requires refresh token management). Account ID required in URL for all requests — must discover/store during auth. Invoice creation is not idempotent — retrying creates duplicates. Webhooks for invoice/payment events. Rate limits per plan tier. GDPR compliance important for financial data. Pagination uses offset-based approach.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth2 with scopes for accounting data (invoices, expenses, clients). No API key option — OAuth2 required. FreshBooks maintained. No formal compliance certifications publicly listed beyond GDPR. OAuth refresh tokens via secure storage. Scope granularity allows read-only access for agent monitoring workflows. Financial data access requires careful scope selection.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable versioned API. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. OAuth token refresh must be implemented for long-running agents. Invoice creation not idempotent — always check for existing invoices before creating. Rate limits per plan — lower tiers restrict concurrent agent operations. Account ID required in URL adds discovery step to setup.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Freshworks customer support platform. API key used as HTTP Basic Auth username with 'X' as password — clean for agents. Rate limits vary by plan (50-400 req/min). Subdomain-specific endpoints (company.freshdesk.com) require configuration. Ticket creation not idempotent — retrying creates duplicate tickets. Good webhook support for event-driven agents. Custom fields require discovery via API before use. Pagination uses page-based approach.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key as Basic Auth — no scope granularity. Freshworks maintained. GDPR compliance. API key via environment variable. Customer support data (PII, contact info) requires careful data handling per applicable regulations. Subdomain-specific API limits exposure to one account. No read-only API key option — same key for read and write.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Rate limits vary significantly by plan (50-400/min) — design for lowest tier. Ticket creation not idempotent — check for existing tickets before creating. Custom field discovery required at startup adds initialization complexity. Attachment upload requires separate multipart request.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Digital experience analytics — session recording, user journey analysis, event data. Simple API key. Data Export API for bulk session data retrieval. User lookup and custom event ingestion available. Rate limits by plan. Export jobs are async — agents must poll completion. PII handling critical — FullStory captures user behavior including potentially sensitive interactions. Data residency options available on enterprise plans.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. FullStory maintained. SOC2/GDPR compliance for behavioral analytics platform. API key via environment variable. Session recording data contains PII (user interactions, pages visited, potentially form data) — strict data governance required. Data masking configuration is critical for compliance. Enterprise data residency available.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Async export jobs require polling — implement with appropriate timeouts. GET operations idempotent. Data export rate limits apply for large-scale data access. Session data may have retention limits per plan — historical data access may be unavailable beyond retention window.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for Galway, Ireland public transit data (quality 42). Wraps real-time bus arrival data from Irish transit APIs. Very limited documentation. API key required. Rate limits from upstream transit API — not published. Real-time data quality dependent on RTPI (Real Time Passenger Information) system. Niche geographic scope (Galway city only). Limited error handling for transit API failures. Not suitable for production agent workflows outside of specific Galway transit use cases.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS to transit API. API key auth. No scope granularity. Unknown dependency hygiene. API key via environment variable. Read-only public transit data — minimal security risk. No compliance certifications. Community-maintained with unknown review standards.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA — depends on Irish RTPI system availability. Unknown version. No documented change policy. Read operations idempotent. Transit API data quality varies by time of day and service disruptions. GTFS-RT data freshness limited by transit authority update frequency. Very niche use case limits community maintenance activity.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Google Cloud Functions management API — deploy, invoke, manage serverless functions. Service account with IAM is standard for agents. Application Default Credentials (ADC) simplifies local development. Function invocation via HTTP endpoint (separate from management API). Rate limits per project documented. Async deployment requires polling operation status. Env vars and secrets managed via Secret Manager integration. Cloud Functions Gen2 (based on Cloud Run) preferred for new deployments.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Service account + IAM with fine-grained per-resource permissions. roles/cloudfunctions.developer or custom roles for least privilege. Google-maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/FedRAMP/HIPAA compliance. Service account JSON via Secret Manager. IAM conditions for time-based or resource-based access control. VPC Service Controls for network perimeter. Excellent security posture as GCP service.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides (Gen1 to Gen2). Retry guidance documented. Async deployment operations require polling via Operations API. ADC simplifies token management. Rate limits per project documented. Function cold starts affect invocation latency — not a management API concern. Gen2 migration path well-documented.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server — this is the Google Cloud Python client library covering all GCP services. Application Default Credentials (ADC) is the cleanest auth pattern for agents (auto-discovers credentials from environment). Multi-service support with consistent patterns across all GCP APIs. Extensive documentation with code examples for every service. gcloud auth application-default login for local dev. Rate limits per service — consult individual service docs. Client library handles token refresh automatically.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced across all GCP services. ADC + service account + OAuth2 options. Fine-grained IAM scopes per service and resource. Google-maintained with active security updates. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/FedRAMP/HIPAA across GCP platform. ADC via GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS env var (service account JSON). Workload Identity Federation for keyless auth in GKE/Cloud Run. Excellent security architecture across all services.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA across services. Stable versioned client library with automated release management. Excellent migration guides across major versions. Built-in retry with exponential backoff. ADC auto-refreshes tokens. Per-service rate limits — consult individual service quotas. Batch operations available for efficiency. Consistent error handling patterns across all GCP services via googleapiclient.errors.
